paper_title: ArtifactNet: Detecting AI-Generated Music via Forensic Residual Physics

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We present ArtifactNet, a lightweight framework that detects AI-generated music by reframing the problem as forensic physics -- extracting and analyzing the physical artifacts that neural audio codecs inevitably imprint on generated audio. A bounded-mask UNet (ArtifactUNet, 3.6M parameters) extracts codec residuals from magnitude spectrograms, which are then decomposed via HPSS into 7-channel forensic features for classification by a compact CNN (0.4M parameters; 4.0M total). We introduce ArtifactBench, a multi-generator evaluation benchmark comprising 6,183 tracks (4,383 AI from 22 generators and 1,800 real from 6 diverse sources). Each track is tagged with bench_origin for fair zero-shot evaluation. On the unseen test partition (n=2,263), ArtifactNet achieves F1 = 0.9829 with FPR = 1.49%, compared to CLAM (F1 = 0.7576, FPR = 69.26%) and SpecTTTra (F1 = 0.7713, FPR = 19.43%) evaluated under identical conditions with published checkpoints. Codec-aware training (4-way WAV/MP3/AAC/Opus augmentation) further reduces cross-codec probability drift by 83% (Delta = 0.95 -> 0.16), resolving the primary codec-invariance failure mode. These results establish forensic physics -- direct extraction of codec-level artifacts -- as a more generalizable and parameter-efficient paradigm for AI music detection than representation learning, using 49x fewer parameters than CLAM and 4.8x fewer than SpecTTTra.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: ArtifactNet: Detecting AI-Generated Music via Forensic Residual Physics Heewon Oh Intrect / MARTE Lab, Dongguk University, Seoul, South Korea heewon.oh@intrect.io

sections.title: ABSTRACT
sections.content: We present ArtifactNet, lightweight framework that detects AI-generated music by reframing the problem as forensic physics extracting and analyzing the physical artifacts that neural audio codecs inevitably imprint on generated audio. bounded-mask UNet (ArtifactUNet, 3.6M parameters) extracts codec residuals from magnitude spectrograms, which are then decomposed via HPSS into 7-channel forensic features for classification by compact CNN (0.4M parameters; 4.0M total). We introduce ArtifactBench, multigenerator evaluation benchmark comprising 6,183 tracks (4,383 AI from 22 generators and 1,800 real from 6 diverse sources). Each track is tagged with bench_origin for fair zeroshot evaluation. On the unseen test partition (n=2,263), ArtifactNet achieves F1 = 0.9829 with FPR = 1.49%, compared to CLAM (F1 = 0.7576, FPR = 69.26%) and SpecTTTra (F1 = 0.7713, FPR = 19.43%) evaluated under identical conditions with published checkpoints. Codec- (4-way WAV/MP3/AAC/Opus aware augmentation) further reduces cross-codec probability drift by 83% (Δ = 0.95 0.16), resolving the primary codecinvariance failure mode. These results establish forensic physics direct extraction of codec-level artifacts as more generalizable and parameter-efficient paradigm for AI music detection than representation learning, using 49 fewer parameters than CLAM and 4.8 fewer than SpecTTTra. training 1. INTRODUCTION The proliferation of AI-generated music on streaming platforms [1] with over 50,000 fully AI-generated tracks uploaded daily [1] demands detection methods grounded in the physics of audio generation rather than surface-level pattern matching. Current approaches fall into two paradigms: representation learning, which finetunes large pretrained encoders on labeled data [2, 3], and autoencoder fingerprinting, which learns codec-specific reconstruction patterns [4, 5]. Both achieve high indistribution accuracy but share fundamental limitation: they fail on unseen generators. Afchar et al. [4] demonstrate this concretely. Training classifiers on autoencoder reconstruction error yields 99.8% accuracy, yet the authors themselves identify structural generalization failure when encountering unseen decoders. They raise key question: Can AI-generated music be identified regardless of its content? They also note that compression codecs and genre distribution remain major unresolved challenges [4]. We answer this question affirmatively. Our key insight is that all commercial AI music generators (Suno, Udio, Stable Audio, MusicGen, Riffusion, etc.) share common physical constraint: their neural audio codecs employ Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ), which maps continuous audio representations onto discrete codebook vectors through iterative quantization [6, 11, 12, 16]. This process is irreversible the quantization gap between the continuous latent and its nearest codebook entry cannot be recovered. When source separation model trained exclusively on human music encounters AI-generated audio, this gap manifests as anomalously large and structured reconstruction residuals phenomenon we term forensic residual amplification. Our contributions are: (1) Forensic physics reframing we demonstrate that AI music detection is fundamentally problem of detecting irreversible RVQ artifacts, explaining why codec-specific approaches fail while physics-based approaches generalize; (2) ArtifactUNet with bounded mask steering 3.6M-parameter STFT masking network trained via two-phase knowledge distillation; (3) HPSS as forensic feature the first application of Harmonic-Percussive Source Separation source separation residuals as forensic signal; and (4) ArtifactBench multi-generator evaluation benchmark comprising 6,183 tracks across 22 generators with bench_origin tagging for fair zero-shot evaluation, revealing that competing approaches degrade to F1 = 0.75760.7713 under identical evaluation conditions. to 2. RELATED WORK

sections.title:
2.1  Autoencoder  Fingerprinting  and  Representation 
Learning
sections.content: Afchar et al. [4, 5] pioneer AI music detection by training classifiers its to distinguish original audio autoencoder reconstruction, achieving 99.8% accuracy from while explicitly acknowledging four caveats for deployed detectors: robustness to audio manipulation, generalisation to unseen decoders, calibration, and interpretability. They further identify compression codec and genre distribution as potential confounders. CLAM [2] proposes dualstream architecture combining MERT [10] and Wav2Vec2 encoders (194M parameters) with contrastive triplet loss, achieving F1 = 0.925 on the MoM OOD test set and F1 = 0.993 on SONICS. SpecTTTra [3] models long-range temporal dependencies and achieves F1 = 0.97 on the SONICS in-distribution test set, but degrades to 5068% the MoM F1 on out-of-distribution generators benchmark [2]. These approaches learn what AI music sounds like rather than why it is physically different, limiting generalization when the generation style changes. Recent surveys [18, 22] confirm this generalization challenge as the central open problem in audio deepfake detection. in

sections.title: 2.2 Neural Codec Bottleneck
sections.content: Modern AI music generators universally employ neural audio codecs (EnCodec [6], DAC [11]), building on the VQ-VAE framework [12] and SoundStream [15], with Residual Vector Quantization as their audio tokenization backend. RVQ approximates continuous latent vectors through cascaded codebook lookups: q₁ + q₂ + + qN, where each qᵢ is the nearest codebook entry for the cumulative residual. With finite codebooks (typically 1,024 entries 832 layers), this introduces systematic, irreversible information loss particularly in highfrequency content and fine temporal structure. This shared bottleneck is the physical basis for generator-agnostic detection: regardless of upstream architecture whether autoregressive (MusicGen [16], MusicLM [20], Jukebox [25]), diffusion-based (Stable Audio [26], Riffusion), or latent diffusion (AudioLDM [28]), the audio must pass through RVQ, leaving universal forensic signature.

sections.title: 2.3 Audio Forensics and Source Separation
sections.content: Source separation via Demucs v4 [7, 23] has not been explored as forensic tool. HPSS [8] is standard in MIR but has not been applied to forensic residual analysis. Our work bridges audio forensics and source separation: rather than using these tools for their intended purpose, we repurpose them as forensic amplifiers. The closest analog in image forensics is the shift from pixel-level classifiers to Diffusion Noise Features [9] physics-based approaches that target generation mechanisms generalize where stylelike AASIST [21] (evaluated on based approaches fail. In speech anti-spoofing, end-to-end approaches such as RawNet2 [27] and graph-based architectures the ASVspoof 2021 benchmark [17]) have shown similar benefits from targeting generation artifacts rather than content, though these methods address voice-only signals and do not transfer directly to full music mixtures [18, 27]. 3. METHOD

sections.title: 3.1 Architecture Overview
sections.content: ArtifactNet processes audio through three stages: (1) forensic residual extraction via ArtifactUNet, (2) 7-channel HPSS feature computation, and (3) classification via lightweight CNN with song-level verdict aggregation. The complete pipeline requires 4.0M parameters. Fig. 1. ArtifactNet pipeline overview. Audio is processed through ArtifactUNet for forensic residual extraction, HPSSbased 7-channel feature computation, and CNN classification.

sections.title: 3.2 ArtifactUNet: Bounded Mask Residual Extraction
sections.content: Design principle. Unlike direct residual generation (which risks identity mapping shortcuts), ArtifactUNet, following the U-Net encoder-decoder architecture [13], predicts multiplicative mask on the input STFT magnitude: = X, where is the predicted mask and is the input magnitude spectrogram. Bounded mask. The mask is constrained to [0, 0.5] via sigmoid scaling: = 0.5 σ(z). This enforces the physical prior that forensic artifacts constitute at most half the total signal energy at any time-frequency bin, preventing shortcut solutions that pass through the entire signal. Two-phase training. Phase 1 (Knowledge Distillation [14]): ArtifactUNet learns the structural form of source separation residuals by minimizing L1 + multi-resolution STFT loss against Demucs v4 [7] residuals (built on the original waveform Demucs framework [23]) as teacher targets, transferring the shape of forensic residuals without requiring the 42M-parameter Demucs at inference time. Phase 2 (Frozen Classifier Steering): with the downstream CNN frozen, ArtifactUNet is fine-tuned end-to-end via through BCE classification differentiable mel-spectrogram transform. This steers residual extraction toward discriminatively optimal features, eliminating oscillation observed in joint training. loss backpropagated encoder-decoder with [13] Architecture. U-Net GatedResidualBlock bottleneck, 3.6M parameters, 16 MB on disk. Codec-Aware Training. Training on 4-way codec variants (WAV, MP3 128kbps, AAC 128kbps, Opus 128kbps) teaches the UNet to suppress codec-induced artifacts in the residual, preventing the network from lossy compression signatures as forensic encoding evidence. Without codec-aware training, MP3 encoding flips real music to AI-positive (FPR 98.7%), while AAC encoding flips AI tracks to real-negative the original UNet produces codec-dependent residuals. Codec-aware training reduces the cross-codec probability delta from 0.95 to 0.16 (83%), eliminating this asymmetry. See Section 4.7 for the full ablation. Fig. 2. Baseline vs codec-aware UNet probability distributions across 4 codecs for Real tracks. 3.3 7-Channel Forensic Features We apply HPSS to the extracted residual magnitude spectrogram, decomposing it into harmonic (H) and percussive (P) components via median filtering along time and frequency axes. Combined with temporal derivatives and spectral statistics, this yields 7-channel input representation: Table 1. Seven-Channel Forensic Feature Summary Channel mel_res mel_H mel_P Description Forensic Rationale Mel-scaled residual spectrum Base forensic signal Harmonic component (HPSS) Percussive component (HPSS) Pitched content leak from failed separation Transient energy leak Δ First temporal Frame-to-frame derivative artifact dynamics

sections.title: Tracks  Class  Purpose
sections.content: Δ² Second derivative temporal Acceleration artifact patterns of AIME (9 generators) 2,790 AI hp_ratio log(H/P) ratio Harmonic-percussive balance of artifacts spectral_flux Frame-to-frame spectral change Temporal instability of codec artifacts HPSS has been used extensively for music analysis [8] but never applied to source separation residuals as forensic tool. The harmonic component captures pitched content that the separation model failed to attribute to any stem an anomaly structurally larger for AI-generated audio due to the RVQ bottleneck's impact on harmonic fine structure. The percussive component captures leaked transient energy from rhythmic elements that violate the separation model's learned temporal priors.

sections.title: 3.4 Classification and Song-Level Verdict
sections.content: Segment-level. compact CNN (3 Conv-BN-ReLUPool blocks, AdaptiveAvgPool, FC layers; 0.4M parameters) processes 4-second segments, outputting P(AI) [0, 1]. Song-level median verdict (CNN-only baseline): median segment probability thresholded at 0.5. 4. sections.title: 4.2 Comparison with Prior Work
sections.content: ArtifactUNet Phase 1 trains on paired (audio, Demucs residual) data via knowledge distillation. Phase 2 and the 7-channel CNN train on (audio, label) pairs with stratified sampling across the 28 source subsets in Table 2. Phase 3 (codec-aware) training applies 4-way codec augmentation (WAV, MP3 128kbps, AAC 128kbps, Opus 128kbps) to every training sample, so the model sees each track under all four codecs in the same batch. This codec-aware regime intentionally exposes the model to MP3/AAC/Opus real music addressing the earlier failure mode (98.7% FPR on FMA [30] mp3 archives) where the network previously confused lossy codec residuals with neural-codec artifacts. Hard-negative mining extends the real music set with FMA mp3 corpora and curated YouTube production tracks to suppress remaining false positives. Table 2. Training data composition for ArtifactNet v9.4 (20,374 tracks across 8 source groups, stratified over 28 benchtaxonomy subsets, 4-way codec augmentation). Table 3. Performance on ArtifactBench (n=2,263, bench_origin=test, all models unseen, τ=0.5). See 4.6 for FPR and AUC.

sections.title: Precision  Recall
sections.content: ArtifactNet (ours) 4.0M 0.9829 0.9905 0.9755 CLAM [2] 194M 0.7576 0.6674 SpecTTTra [3] 19M 0. 0.8519 0.8761 0.7046 All three models are evaluated on ArtifactBench continuously-updated benchmark we constructed for this work under identical conditions: same audio files, same preprocessing, threshold τ = 0.5 (consistent with the authors' inference code). For fair zero-shot comparison, we restrict evaluation to the test partition (bench_origin=test, n=2,263), unseen by all three models during training. ArtifactNet achieves the highest performance across all metrics (F1 = 0.9829 vs. 0.7576 for CLAM and 0.7713 for SpecTTTra), while using 49 fewer parameters than CLAM and 4.8 fewer than SpecTTTra. Notably, CLAM exhibits 69.3% FPR on real music including 67% FPR on the MoM real subset from which CLAM's own training data was drawn 46 larger error rate than ArtifactNet's 1.5%, despite CLAM being trained on the MoM dataset from which portion of our real test sources derive.

This convergence confirms that the forensic signal originates at the shared neural codec bottleneck, providing independent physical validation of the detection mechanism. sections.title:
4.4  Bandwidth  Fingerprinting:  Physical  Evidence  of 
RVQ
sections.content: To investigate what physical property of the residual carries discriminative information, we measured effective bandwidth of source separation residuals (n = 94 tracks: 50 AI, 44 real). AI residuals exhibit an effective bandwidth of 291 Hz versus 1,996 Hz for human music 6.9 difference providing direct physical evidence of the RVQ bottleneck's acoustic footprint. Table 5. Effective Bandwidth by Generator Generator Suno v3.5 Riffusion Stable Audio Effective BW (Hz) Architecture 170 219 237 Proprietary Diffusion Latent diffusion F1 0.9950 1-pass 0. TPR FPR 99.0% 94.0% 0.0% 2.7% Original Demucs laundering Single-pass Demucs separation reduces F1 from 0.9950 to 0.9592. The laundered AI vs. real AUC remains 0.9651 in the 103-dimensional feature space, indicating that while one-pass laundering degrades performance, the forensic signal is not fully destroyed. Augmented retraining with Demucs-separated examples is incorporated in ArtifactNet training data.

sections.title: 4.6 ArtifactBench: Multi-Generator Fair Evaluation
sections.content: Existing benchmarks evaluate AI music detection on narrow generator distributions: SONICS [3] covers 5 generators (Suno/Udio variants), MoM [2] covers 6 generators, and FakeMusicCaps [19] focuses on attribution rather than out-of-distribution generalization. High scores on in-distribution memorization rather than genuine forensic capability. We introduce ArtifactBench, multi-generator evaluation benchmark designed to expose this distinction. these benchmarks may reflect ArtifactBench v1 comprises 6,183 tracks: 4,383 AIgenerated from 22 distinct generators (Suno v3/v3.5/v4, Udio v1/v1.5, MusicGen, Stable Audio, Riffusion, Diffrythm, Yue, JEN-1, and others) and 1,800 real tracks from 6 diverse sources (SONICS real, MoM real, FMA hard-negatives, YouTube hard-negatives, professional WAV recordings, hand-curated lossless). Each track is tagged with bench_origin = test or train; for fair zero-shot comparison we restrict evaluation to bench_origin=test (n=2,263: 1,388 AI + 875 real, unseen by all three models). The at published with huggingface.co/datasets/intrect/artifactbench-v1 audio bytes embedded as Parquet shards for full reproducibility. benchmark is We define sanity check protocol: for each of 22 AI generators and 6 real sources (28 subsets), model PASSES on an AI subset if TPR 90% ( 60% for the harder Stable Audio v1/v2 subsets), and PASSES on real subset if FPR 5%. model FAILS subset when it falls below these production-grade operating thresholds indicating that the model would be unusable on that specific generator or audio domain in deployment. Three-way comparison on ArtifactBench (Table 3 above) reveals asymmetric failure modes: ArtifactNet maintains balanced precision and recall across all 28 source subsets, CLAM exhibits high recall (87.6%) but catastrophic precision collapse (66.7%) due to systematic overprediction of AI on real music, and SpecTTTra exhibits the inverse pattern high precision (85.2%) but low recall to detect out-of-distribution (70.5%) generators. The 0.9974 AUC for ArtifactNet versus 0.7031 for CLAM and 0.8460 for SpecTTTra indicates that ArtifactNet's superior performance is intrinsic to its forensic discriminator rather than threshold-dependent. failure from Fig. 3. CLAM's high FPR (69.3%) reflects false-positive bias on out-ofdistribution real music; SpecTTTra's low recall (70.5%) reflects failure to detect generators outside its training distribution. systematic

sections.title: 4.7 UNet Codec-Aware Training Ablation
sections.content: the network encodes critical limitation of the original (Phase 2) UNet is codec sensitivity: lossy compression artifacts as forensic evidence, producing codec-dependent residuals. We ablate this by comparing Phase 2 and codecaware (Phase 3) UNet variants across four codecs. Phase 2 UNet results. For real music, the cross-codec probability delta is Δ = 0.95: WAV input yields low P(AI) as expected, but MP3 encoding shifts the mean probability to near 1.0 (FPR 98.7%), while AAC and Opus produce intermediate values. For AI-generated tracks, the pattern inverts asymmetrically (Δ = 0.72): AAC encoding reduces P(AI) substantially, creating false negatives. Codec-aware (Phase 3) UNet results. Training on 4-way codec variants collapses the distribution: Real Δ drops from 0.95 to 0.16 (83%), AI Δ from 0.72 to 0.14 (81%). The codec-aware UNet learns to suppress codec-induced spectral artifacts in the residual, producing consistent forensic features regardless of the input codec. This eliminates the primary failure mode where MP3 encoding flips real music to AI-positive.

sections.title: 4.8 Receiver Operating Characteristic Analysis
sections.content: Fig. 7 shows the ROC curve and F1-vs-threshold plot for ArtifactNet on ArtifactBench. At the operating point FPR 5%, ArtifactNet achieves TPR = 99.1%. The F1 score remains above 0.98 across thresholds τ [0.05, 0.9], demonstrating that the system is not sensitive to threshold selection desirable property for deployment where require different FPR different guarantees. stakeholders may Fig. 7. ROC curve and F1 score vs. threshold for ArtifactNet on ArtifactBench. 5. DISCUSSION

sections.title: 5.1 Why Forensic Physics Generalizes
sections.content: The central result that 4.0M-parameter system outperforms 194M-parameter alternative (CLAM) and 19M-parameter alternative (SpecTTTra) by F1 margins of +0.23 and +0.21 respectively has straightforward explanation. Representation learning approaches learn what AI music sounds like from their training distribution. When the sound changes (new generator, new style), [29]. learned ArtifactNet detects why AI music is physically different: all generators share the RVQ bottleneck, and the source representations unreliable become separation prior amplifies the resulting quantization gap into measurable forensic signal regardless of architectural details. This parallels the shift in image forensics from pixel-level classifiers to physics-based Diffusion Noise Features [9], echoing the broader observation that physicsgrounded features outperform learned representations under distribution shift in image classification [31]. fundamental information-theoretic An important corollary: this detection methodology targets constraint. Continuous-to-discrete quantization is irreversible generators cannot remove RVQ artifacts [12] without abandoning neural codecs entirely. This asymmetry makes the forensic signal structurally robust to generator improvements, unlike style-based detectors that can be circumvented by changing the generation aesthetic.

sections.title: 5.2 Limitations
sections.content: Full-bandwidth requirement. Our forensic features operate on 44.1kHz residuals targeting high-frequency RVQ artifacts. Benchmarks distributed at reduced sample rates (e.g., 16kHz) attenuate the forensic signal. This is design trade-off: full-bandwidth analysis enables the cross-generator physical generalization at the cost of requiring lossless or highquality input. precision drives that FPR on challenging MP3 corpora. The original (Phase 2) UNet exhibits 98.7% FPR on low-quality MP3 archives (FMA [30]) due to codec-induced residual artifacts mimicking AI signatures. Codec-aware UNet training (Phase 3) reduces this to 8.0%, addressing the primary limitation of the original system. Further reduction is expected with expanded codec-augmented training data. Udio latest-generation detection. Latest-generation Udio tracks achieve TPR = 87%, lower than other generators. Analysis reveals that quiet and transition segments produce harmonic-percussive ratios converging with real music patterns, reducing per-segment confidence. Future work on auxiliary scalar features (e.g., harmonic-percussive ratio statistics) at the CNN input may improve detection of perceptually converging generators. Adversarial laundering. Single-pass Demucs laundering reduces TPR to 94.0%. While residual forensic signal remains detectable (AUC 0.9651), multi-pass or targeted adversarial attacks merit further investigation. Proactive approaches such as audio watermarking [24] offer complementary defense, though they require generator cooperation and cannot protect against already-deployed systems. 6. CONCLUSION We presented ArtifactNet, demonstrating that AI music detection is most effectively approached as forensic physics problem. The proposed system bounded-mask UNet for residual extraction, HPSS-based 7-channel feature decomposition, and compact CNN classification achieves F1 = 0.9829 (AUC = 0.9974) on ArtifactBench (2,263 tracks, 22 AI generators and 6 real music sources, zero training overlap with the test partition) with only 4.0M parameters. Codec-aware UNet training resolves the primary codec-invariance limitation, reducing cross-codec probability delta by 83%. ArtifactBench, our multigenerator evaluation benchmark with per-generator stratified coverage and zero training overlap, reveals that competing approaches suffer catastrophic performance degradation on out-of-distribution generators: CLAM achieves F1 = 0.7576 and SpecTTTra F1 = 0.7713 under identical conditions, compared to their originally reported scores of F1 0.92. pre-compiled ONNX inference build of the full pipeline (UNet + HPSS + 7-channel CNN, endto-end) is available at huggingface.co/intrect/artifactnet for evaluation and reproducibility (CC BY-NC 4.0); training code and raw weights are not publicly released. ArtifactBench v1, with full audio bytes embedded as at Parquet huggingface.co/datasets/intrect/artifactbench (CC BY-NC 4.0). Patent applications covering the bounded-mask residual extraction and codec-invariant training methods are pending (KR + PCT). available shards, is Afchar et al. [4] asked whether AI-generated music can be detected independently of musical content. Our answer: yes by listening to what the music cannot contain, rather than what it does.
